text: The laparoscopic view reveals an operable lesion in the liver, confirming the feasibility of proceeding with the planned surgical resection.
The video shows a large centrally located tumor in the liver with a visible satellite nodule.
The surgeon observes the liver, noting its fatty appearance and the small size of the left lobe.
The surgeon uses the glissonian pedicle approach to dissect the pedicle, focusing on the central area of the liver.
The surgeon exposes, dissects, and isolates the right pedicle using a vascular sling, followed by the dissection of the right anterior and posterior pedicles.
The surgeon marks the line of demarcation on the liver's right side, delineating between segment VI-VII and segment V-VIII.
The surgeon performs parenchymal transection using an ultrasonic aspirator, carefully separating the liver tissue.
The surgeon clips and divides smaller vessels, while larger vessels are carefully dissected, ligated, and divided within the liver tissue.
The surgeon uses bipolar diathermy to achieve hemostasis on the cut surface of the liver, ensuring control of bleeding following the resection.
The surgeon continues the transduction of liver parenchyma, progressing deeper and cranially, using an ultrasonic aspirator to carefully separate tissue layers.
Near the hilum, the surgeon dissects and exposes the right anterior pedicle, revealing its anatomical position and relation to surrounding structures.
The surgeon creates a plane between the right hepatic vein and the middle hepatic vein as the transaction progresses cranially, revealing the spatial relationship between these vascular structures.
The exposed right hepatic vein is visible on the right side of the surgical field, with noticeable pulsations.
The surgeon places stay sutures on the left side to facilitate the left-sided transection during the liver resection procedure.
The surgeon uses diathermy to divide the bridge of tissue between the left and right lobes of the liver, separating these anatomical structures.
The surgeon marks the line of transduction on the liver surface on the left side using diathermy, creating a visible line for the planned resection.
The surgeon performs parenchymal transection using an ultrasonic aspirator, visibly separating liver tissue in the surgical field.
The surgeon dissects the Segment IV pedicle, ligates it on both sides, and divides it, completing the separation of this vascular structure.
The surgeon continues the parenchymal transduction, advancing further cranially on the liver surface.
The surgeon creates a transection plane between the middle hepatic vein and the left hepatic vein, using precise dissection techniques to separate these vascular structures.
The surgeon carefully dissects and exposes the middle hepatic vein on the cranial aspect, placing a sling around it for control.
The surgeon clamps and divides the right entry pedicle during the liver resection procedure.
The surgeon oversews the stump of the right entry pedicle using five-0 prolene sutures in two layers, securing the divided pedicle.
The surgeon completes the remaining parenchymal transection, finalizing the separation of liver tissue along the established transection plane.
The surgeon removes the sling from the middle hepatic vein, clamps it, and divides the vein, completing the transection between the middle and left hepatic veins.
The surgeon removes the liver specimen following the completion of the resection, with the specimen visibly separated from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon oversews the stump of the middle hepatic vein using two layers of 5-0 Prolene sutures.
The video shows the transected cut surfaces of the left and right liver sections following the resection.
The right hepatic vein is visible on the right transected surface, showing distinct pulsations.
The video shows segments six and seven, as well as segments two and three of the liver, following the resection.
The main portal pedicle is visible in the surgical field, showing its anatomical position and structure.
The right pedicle is visible in the surgical field following the liver resection.
The stump of the divided right anterior pedicle is visible on the transected liver surface.
